deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Per-customer advisory lock via lockr inside an Ent transaction (transactionForInvoiceManipulation wraps customer-mutating ops)
deep_findings.evidence: CreateStandardInvoiceFromGatheringLines (lines 621-834) is exported on billing.Service (service.go:119) and performs many customer-mutating writes (adapter.CreateInvoice, UpdateGatheringInvoice, HardDeleteGatheringInvoiceLines, updateInvoice) plus publisher.Publish, but never calls transactionForInvoiceManipulation or even transaction.Run itself.
deep_findings.message: An exported customer-mutating Service method relies entirely on its caller (InvoicePendingLines) already holding the per-customer advisory lock + tx, so any direct external caller bypasses both UpsertCustomerLock/LockCustomerForUpdate and the atomic transaction boundary.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Per-customer advisory lock via lockr inside an Ent transaction (billing.Service.WithLock / transactionForInvoiceManipulation)
deep_findings.evidence: RecalculateGatheringInvoices (lines 1029-1072) mutates the customer's gathering invoices via adapter.UpdateGatheringInvoice and DeleteGatheringInvoices inside only transaction.RunWithNoValue, without UpsertCustomerLock/LockCustomerForUpdate (transactionForInvoiceManipulation).
deep_findings.message: A customer-scoped gathering-invoice mutation runs without the per-customer advisory lock, so it can race against concurrent CreatePendingInvoiceLines/InvoicePendingLines on the same customer that DO take the lock.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Invoice / Charge state machine (never mutate Invoice.Status directly; drive transitions through the stateless InvoiceStateMachine)
deep_findings.evidence: SimulateInvoice (line 975) does `invoice.Status = billing.StandardInvoiceStatusDraftInvalid` and sets invoice.StatusDetails.Failed = true directly instead of going through the state machine's FireAndActivate / TriggerFailed.
deep_findings.message: Invoice.Status is mutated directly to derive the simulated draft.invalid outcome, duplicating the state machine's failed-state logic outside it (in-memory only, but erodes the single-status-writer invariant the state machine guarantees).

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: entutils.TransactingRepo context-propagated Ent transactions / atomic multi-step mutation
deep_findings.evidence: ProvisionDefaultBillingProfile (lines 334-384) reads GetDefaultProfile, branches on profile==nil, then calls CreateProfile, all outside any enclosing transaction.Run; the existence check and the create are not atomic.
deep_findings.message: A check-then-act default-profile provisioning runs without a surrounding transaction, so two concurrent provisioners can both pass the nil check and attempt to create competing default profiles.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Domain errors returned as billing typed sentinels mapped by the billing errorEncoder (httpdriver/errors.go)
deep_findings.evidence: ResolveStripeAppIDFromBillingProfile (lines 569-625) mixes models.NewGenericNotFoundError(...) with plain fmt.Errorf('apps are not expanded ...') / fmt.Errorf('billing profile apps are not expanded') for domain conditions; the billing errorEncoder only maps billing.NotFoundError/ValidationError/UpdateAfterDeleteError/AppError, not models.Generic* nor plain errors.
deep_findings.message: Domain failure conditions are returned as plain fmt.Errorf and as models.Generic* errors that the billing errorEncoder does not match, so they fall through to HTTP 500 instead of the intended 4xx.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Per-customer advisory lock via lockr inside an Ent transaction (transactionForInvoiceManipulation)
deep_findings.evidence: CreatePendingInvoiceLines (lines 26-71) calls customerService.GetCustomer and reads cust.DeletedAt to validate line periods before entering transactionForInvoiceManipulation at line 73; the deleted-at guard is evaluated outside the customer lock window.
deep_findings.message: The customer soft-delete guard for pending lines is evaluated before the per-customer lock is acquired, so a concurrent customer deletion between the read and the locked write can let lines be created against a just-deleted customer.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() and caller ctx everywhere (openmeter/CLAUDE.md pattern; reconciler/CLAUDE.md decision 'clock.Now() for testable time' + anti-pattern 'Using time.Now() instead of clock.Now()')
deep_findings.evidence: reconciler.go:212 ReconcileSubscription calls `r.subscriptionSync.SyncByID(ctx, subsID, time.Now())`, passing the sync 'asOf' horizon from time.Now(). The same file already uses clock.Now() for every other temporal value (lines 114, 115, 149, 239), and pkg/clock is imported (line 15). The reconciler's own CLAUDE.md explicitly lists this as an anti-pattern and a watch-for.
deep_findings.message: ReconcileSubscription is the single outlier in reconciler.go using time.Now() instead of clock.Now() for the sync asOf time, defeating clock.FreezeTime in deterministic billing reconciler tests. clock is already imported and used elsewhere in this file. Replace time.Now() with clock.Now().

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() and caller ctx everywhere (openmeter/CLAUDE.md); subscriptionsync/service/CLAUDE.md ('thin Kafka-event handlers ... HandleInvoiceCreation uses clock.Now()')
deep_findings.evidence: sync.go:60 HandleSubscriptionSyncEvent calls `s.synchronizeSubscriptionAndInvoiceCustomer(ctx, ..., time.Now())`. The same file uses clock.Now() three times (lines 301, 311, 319) and imports pkg/clock (line 18). Sibling event handlers in handlers.go use clock.Now() consistently (handlers.go:19,70,81).
deep_findings.message: HandleSubscriptionSyncEvent passes time.Now() as the sync asOf timestamp while the sibling handlers (HandleCancelledEvent/HandleInvoiceCreation in handlers.go) and the rest of sync.go use clock.Now(). This inconsistency breaks time-frozen sync tests for the SubscriptionSync event path. Use clock.Now().

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() and caller ctx everywhere for deterministic billing tests (openmeter/CLAUDE.md; AGENTS.md clock.FreezeTime testing rule)
deep_findings.evidence: advance.go:82 `DraftUntilLTE: lo.ToPtr(time.Now())` and advance.go:97 `CollectionAtLTE: lo.ToPtr(time.Now())` use time.Now() to compute the auto-advance / collection due cutoffs that select which invoices are eligible. The package imports "time" (line 9), not pkg/clock.
deep_findings.message: The auto-advancer's eligibility cutoffs (DraftUntilLTE, CollectionAtLTE) use time.Now() instead of clock.Now(), so a frozen test clock cannot deterministically control which invoices the advancer picks up. Switch these billing-time cutoffs to clock.Now().

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() and caller ctx everywhere for deterministic billing tests (openmeter/CLAUDE.md; AGENTS.md clock.FreezeTime testing rule)
deep_findings.evidence: collect.go:146 `CollectionAt: time.Now()` (the collection-due cutoff passed to ListCollectableInvoices) and collect.go:192 `AsOf: time.Now()` (passed into CollectCustomerInvoice -> InvoicePendingLines) both use time.Now(). The package imports "time" (line 10), not pkg/clock.
deep_findings.message: The collect loop's CollectionAt cutoff and per-customer AsOf both use time.Now() rather than clock.Now(), making collection eligibility and the invoicing reference time non-deterministic under a frozen test clock. Use clock.Now() for both.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() and caller ctx everywhere (openmeter/CLAUDE.md); subscriptionsync/service pattern that the sync asOf time is clock.Now()
deep_findings.evidence: worker.go:158, 179, 186 each pass `time.Now()` as the asOf timestamp to `w.subscriptionSync.SyncByViewAndInvoiceCustomer(ctx, view, time.Now())` from the Watermill event handler closures. worker.go imports only "time" (line 8). The subscriptionsync handlers that the worker mirrors (handlers.go) use clock.Now() for the same asOf parameter.
deep_findings.message: The billing worker's subscription-sync event closures feed time.Now() as the sync asOf time into SyncByViewAndInvoiceCustomer, diverging from the clock.Now() convention used by the subscriptionsync service's own handlers and breaking determinism of the event-driven sync path. Use clock.Now().

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() and caller ctx everywhere for deterministic billing/charge tests (openmeter/billing/charges uses clock.Now() ~22x vs 1 time.Now())
deep_findings.evidence: charges/worker/advance/advance.go:51 `now := time.Now()` is used as `AdvanceAfterLTE: now` in ListCustomersToAdvance, selecting which customers' charges are due for advancement. The charges subtree otherwise uses clock.Now() pervasively (22 non-test files vs this single time.Now()); the package imports "time" (line 8), not pkg/clock.
deep_findings.message: The charges auto-advancer computes its AdvanceAfterLTE cutoff with time.Now() while the rest of the charges domain standardizes on clock.Now(), so a frozen test clock cannot control which charges become advance-eligible. Switch to clock.Now().

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: entutils.TransactingRepo context-propagated Ent transactions (TxCreator+TxUser triad) / pitfall ctx-001
deep_findings.evidence: All six methods of featureDBAdapter (CreateFeature:43, UpdateFeature:100, GetByIdOrKey:170, ArchiveFeature:201, HasActiveFeatureForMeter:272, ListFeatures:285) call c.db.* directly. None wrap their body in entutils.TransactingRepo, even though transaction.go provides the Tx/WithTx/Self triad and the adapter's own CLAUDE.md states 'TransactingRepo on every method'. Every sibling adapter (plan/adapter/plan.go, addon/adapter/addon.go, planaddon/adapter/planaddon.go) wraps 5 methods each; feature.go is the lone outlier with zero TransactingRepo calls.
deep_findings.message: The feature Ent adapter never rebinds to the ctx-bound transaction via entutils.TransactingRepo, so every feature write runs on the raw client and silently falls off any caller-supplied transaction. This is the exact ctx-001 pitfall (raw *db.Client ignores the active ctx transaction) and breaks the TxCreator+TxUser triad the package otherwise enforces. Wrap each method body in entutils.TransactingRepo / TransactingRepoWithNoValue like every other productcatalog adapter.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Ent ORM + context-propagated transactions (atomic multi-step mutation) / pitfall ctx-001
deep_findings.evidence: ArchiveFeature (lines 201-269) performs a read-check-then-write across c.db with no transaction: GetByIdOrKey (202), then two separate c.db.Plan.Query()/c.db.Subscription.Query() reference checks (217-248), then c.db.Feature.Update().SetArchivedAt() (260). Because none of these run inside entutils.TransactingRepo, the reference checks and the archive write are not atomic.
deep_findings.message: ArchiveFeature is a multi-step read-then-conditionally-write flow executed without a surrounding transaction (no TransactingRepo). A plan or subscription that references the feature can be created in the window between the reference checks and the SetArchivedAt write, archiving a feature that is in fact still referenced — the partial-write/TOCTOU hazard the TransactingRepo discipline exists to prevent.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Validation and event publishing in the service layer / 'events published inside the transaction.Run closure so a publish failure rolls back the DB write' (productcatalog pattern)
deep_findings.evidence: featureConnector.CreateFeature (258-274), UpdateFeature (278-..., publish at 326-328) and ArchiveFeature (publish at 357-359) call featureRepo write methods then c.publisher.Publish OUTSIDE any transaction.Run. By contrast plan/service/plan.go and addon/service/addon.go publish inside transaction.Run(ctx, s.adapter, fn) (plan.go:157/164, 225/232, 345/352; addon.go:189/196, 270/277) so a publish failure rolls back the DB write.
deep_findings.message: The feature connector publishes Watermill events after the repo write but not inside a transaction.Run closure, so a Publish failure leaves the feature persisted while the event is lost — the precise inconsistency the 'publish inside the transaction' pattern (followed by plan and addon services) is designed to prevent. Combined with the adapter's missing TransactingRepo, there is no transactional boundary around the feature mutation + event at all.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Per-customer pg_advisory_lock via lockr inside an Ent transaction (subscription decision: 'pg-advisory lock per customer for serialization')
deep_findings.evidence: Create (101) calls s.lockCustomer at 132 and Cancel (300) calls it at 310, but Update (196), Delete (249) and Continue (360) never call s.lockCustomer / GetCustomerLock / LockForTX. All three perform multi-row writes: Update/Continue run s.sync() (which sync.go/synchelpers.go confirm does NOT lock), and Delete loops s.deletePhase over every phase then SubscriptionRepo.Delete (277-288). The only lock-like construct in these methods is s.mu.RLock() (212, 267, 381), a Go in-memory mutex guarding the Hooks slice, not the pg advisory lock.
deep_findings.message: Subscription Update, Delete, and Continue write multiple subscription/phase/item rows for one customer without acquiring the per-customer pg_advisory_lock, while Create and Cancel do. This violates the documented decision 'pg-advisory lock per customer for serialization' and the service CLAUDE.md anti-pattern 'Omitting the customer advisory lock for operations that write subscription or item rows', so concurrent edits/deletes/continues for the same customer can race and produce conflicting billing lines or overlapping entitlement grants.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: lockCustomer before any customer-scoped write (workflow decision: 'New mutating methods must call lockCustomer before transaction.Run, or concurrent subscription mutations per customer race')
deep_findings.evidence: Only CreateFromPlan calls s.lockCustomer (subscription.go:27). EditRunning (94) opens transaction.Run then delegates to s.Service.Update (173) — which itself never locks — so no advisory lock is ever taken for the edit. Restore (247) deletes every scheduled subscription via s.Service.Delete (285) and continues via s.Service.Continue (291), both of which are the un-locked service methods, again with no lockCustomer in the workflow. AddAddon (addon.go:21) and ChangeAddonQuantity (addon.go:118) run syncWithAddons -> s.Service.Update with no lockCustomer either.
deep_findings.message: EditRunning, Restore, AddAddon and ChangeAddonQuantity mutate one customer's subscriptions without ever acquiring the per-customer advisory lock, because they delegate to subscription.Service.Update/Delete/Continue which also do not lock. Only CreateFromPlan (and, transitively via Cancel/Create, ChangeToPlan) is serialized. This directly violates the workflow decision and anti-pattern 'Skipping lockCustomer in new mutating workflows for a customer — concurrent mutations race'.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: entutils.TransactingRepo context-propagated Ent transactions (pattern); credit/adapter CLAUDE.md 'TransactingRepo wrapping on every write'; ctx-001 / layer-007-adapter-triad
deep_findings.evidence: grantDBADapter.CreateGrant (grant.go:35-64), VoidGrant (grant.go:75-81) and DeleteOwnerGrants (grant.go:66-72) call g.db.Grant.Create()/.Update() directly on the raw *db.Client and never wrap their bodies in entutils.TransactingRepo / TransactingRepoWithNoValue (note the two literal `// TODO: transaction and locking` comments at lines 36 and 76). The sibling balanceSnapshotRepo in the same package wraps every single method (balance_snapshot.go:29,38,61), and the grant repo does not even implement WithTx-rebinding inside its own methods.
deep_findings.message: The grant adapter is the only repo in openmeter/credit/adapter that does NOT honor the ctx-bound Ent transaction inside its own method bodies. It works today only because the credit connector manually rebinds (m.GrantRepo.WithTx(ctx, tx).CreateGrant(...) in grant.go:104,178), but any caller invoking GrantRepo.CreateGrant/VoidGrant/DeleteOwnerGrants WITHOUT first calling .WithTx silently falls off the transaction onto the autocommit client, producing partial writes. Wrap each method body in entutils.TransactingRepo / TransactingRepoWithNoValue like balanceSnapshotRepo does, so transaction discipline is enforced at the adapter rather than relying on every caller to remember .WithTx.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Ent ORM + context-propagated transactions (key decision: 'atomic multi-step mutation'); openmeter CLAUDE.md 'TransactingRepo wraps every Ent adapter write ... so the ctx-bound transaction is honored'
deep_findings.evidence: entitlementHook.PreDelete (entitlement_hook.go:41) calls h.grantRepo.DeleteOwnerGrants(ctx, ...) directly. This hook is fired from service.DeleteEntitlement inside transaction.Run (entitlement/service/service.go:190 c.hooks.PreDelete then 194 DeleteEntitlement). Because grantDBADapter.DeleteOwnerGrants (credit/adapter/grant.go:66-72) is NOT wrapped in TransactingRepo and the hook does not call .WithTx, the grant soft-delete executes on the non-tx grant client, outside the entitlement-delete transaction.
deep_findings.message: Entitlement soft-delete and its grant cleanup are NOT atomic: the PreDelete hook deletes the owner's grants on a separate (autocommit) connection while the entitlement delete + event publish run in transaction.Run. If the entitlement delete or the EntitlementDeletedEvent publish fails after the hook runs, the transaction rolls back but the grants stay deleted, leaving an entitlement with no grants. Either wrap DeleteOwnerGrants in TransactingRepo at the adapter (so it joins the ctx tx automatically) or have the hook rebind via the ctx transaction driver before mutating.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: 'All times truncated to Granularity (time.Minute) before engine use' (key decision); credit CLAUDE.md 'Granularity truncation on all times'; credit/balance-001 / credit-001
deep_findings.evidence: connector.go:37 defines a configurable Granularity time.Duration field, and VoidGrant correctly truncates with m.Granularity (grant.go:144) while saveSnapshot validates alignment against m.Granularity (helper.go:223-224). But CreateGrant hardcodes `granularity := time.Minute` (grant.go:67) to truncate EffectiveAt and Recurrence.Anchor, instead of using m.Granularity.
deep_findings.message: CreateGrant bypasses the connector's configured Granularity field and hardcodes time.Minute. Today both happen to equal time.Minute (wired in registry/builder/entitlement.go:79), so the bug is latent, but it defeats the single-source-of-truth intent of the Granularity config and is internally inconsistent with VoidGrant (m.Granularity) and saveSnapshot (which validates snap.At against m.Granularity). If Granularity were ever reconfigured, CreateGrant would write a grant whose EffectiveAt is misaligned to the value saveSnapshot enforces. Use m.Granularity in CreateGrant.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: 'All times truncated to Granularity (time.Minute) before engine use' (key decision); credit CLAUDE.md 'Granularity truncation on all times'
deep_findings.evidence: ResetUsageForOwner truncates the reset time with a hardcoded `at := params.At.Truncate(time.Minute)` (balance.go:229) and then passes that `at` through saveSnapshot (balance.go:319-324), where saveSnapshot validates snap.At against m.Granularity (helper.go:223). GetBalanceAt/GetBalanceForPeriod similarly hardcode time.Minute (balance.go:134,157).
deep_findings.message: ResetUsageForOwner (and the GetBalanceAt/GetBalanceForPeriod round-up logic) hardcode time.Minute instead of the connector's configured m.Granularity, the same divergence as CreateGrant. saveSnapshot validates the snapshot timestamp against m.Granularity, so the reset path couples a hardcoded constant to a configurable validator; a future Granularity change would make ResetUsageForOwner produce snapshots saveSnapshot rejects. Drive all of these truncations from m.Granularity.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() over time.Now() (openmeter & entitlement CLAUDE.md anti-pattern: 'Using time.Now() instead of clock.Now() — breaks test determinism with frozen clocks')
deep_findings.evidence: recalculate.go uses time.Now() for domain-logic times: IncludeDeletedAfter window (line 210), the deleted/expired decision `ent.DeletedAt != nil || ... time.Now().After(*ent.ActiveTo)` (line 283), and calculatedAt for the snapshot events sent downstream (lines 309 and 354). entitlementhandler.go does the same for calculatedAt (line 86) and worker.go:227 uses time.Now() as the AsOf fallback fed into the high-watermark filter. The entire credit and metered code paths use clock.Now() (e.g. metered/connector.go:169, credit/adapter/grant.go:86).
deep_findings.message: balanceworker is the only sub-tree in scope that uses time.Now() for domain-meaningful timestamps (snapshot calculatedAt, deleted/expired cutoffs, the AsOf value compared against the high-watermark). The codebase standard (and these CLAUDE.md anti-patterns) is clock.Now() so frozen-clock tests are deterministic; because calculatedAt is recorded as the high-watermark and embedded in published SnapshotEvents, non-frozen time here makes recalculation and watermark dedup non-deterministic under test. Replace the domain-logic time.Now() calls with clock.Now() (pure elapsed-duration metrics timing like calculationStart at entitlementhandler.go:280 and recalculate.go:239 may remain).

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: balance-worker high-watermark filter to skip redundant ClickHouse queries (balance-001; balanceworker/filters CLAUDE.md: HighWatermarkCache treats IsDeleted specially)
deep_findings.evidence: In the live Worker path, processEntitlementEntity records IsDeleted: true when it emits a deleted snapshot (entitlementhandler.go:159-163). In the batch Recalculator, ProcessEntitlements branches via sendEntitlementEvent -> sendEntitlementDeletedEvent for deleted/expired entitlements (recalculate.go:283-284) but then unconditionally calls RecordLastCalculation with only {Entitlement, CalculatedAt} and never sets IsDeleted: true (recalculate.go:265-268).
deep_findings.message: The batch recalculator never marks deleted entitlements as deleted in the high-watermark cache, unlike the live worker (entitlementhandler.go:162). HighWatermarkCache.IsEntitlementInScope short-circuits to out-of-scope when entry.IsDeleted is true (filters/highwatermark.go:69-72); because ProcessEntitlements omits IsDeleted, a batch run that just emitted a delete snapshot leaves the cache entry marked as live, so a subsequent stale event for that deleted entitlement is NOT filtered out and triggers a redundant recalculation. Set IsDeleted on the RecordLastCalculationRequest when sendEntitlementEvent took the deleted branch, mirroring processEntitlementEntity.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: RequestValidatorRegistry (pre-mutation guards) vs ServiceHooks (post-lifecycle callbacks) — separate registries with distinct call sites (customer decision: 'RequestValidatorRegistry and ServiceHooks are separate registries with distinct call sites')
deep_findings.evidence: The exact same business rule — block customer deletion when active entitlements exist — is implemented twice. (1) openmeter/entitlement/validators/customer/validator.go ValidateDeleteCustomer lists entitlements for the customer and returns models.NewGenericConflictError('customer ... still has active entitlements'), wired as a RequestValidator via app/common/entitlement.go:50-55 customerService.RegisterRequestValidator(validator). (2) openmeter/customer/service/hooks/entitlementvalidator.go entitlementValidatorHook.PreDelete calls GetEntitlementsOfCustomer and returns models.NewGenericValidationError('customer has entitlements, please remove them before deleting the customer'), wired as a ServiceHook via app/common/customer.go:114-121 customerService.RegisterHooks(h). Both are registered in cmd/server (wire.go:55 NewCustomerEntitlementValidatorServiceHook and the billing/entitlement validator path). The two guards even disagree on HTTP status: one maps to 409 (GenericConflictError), the other to 400 (GenericValidationError).
deep_findings.message: Two independent implementations of the 'no-delete-with-active-entitlements' guard exist (a RequestValidator and a PreDelete ServiceHook), both registered in cmd/server. They duplicate the entitlement lookup, diverge on the returned error type (409 vs 400), and can drift independently. Consolidate into the single entitlement RequestValidator (entitlement/validators/customer) and delete the customer/service/hooks/entitlementvalidator.go PreDelete hook plus its app/common provider.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: customer-001-requestvalidator-pre-mutation-only / customer decision: 'Pre-mutation validators block before any DB write; post-lifecycle hooks run after success' — pre-mutation blocking validation belongs in RequestValidatorRegistry, not ServiceHooks
deep_findings.evidence: entitlementValidatorHook.PreDelete (lines 48-59) performs purely pre-mutation blocking validation: it reads entitlements and returns models.NewGenericValidationError to abort the delete; it has no side-effect/reaction (the defining purpose of a ServiceHook). It is registered with customerService.RegisterHooks (app/common/customer.go:121), i.e. as a lifecycle hook running inside transaction.Run, whereas customer's design explicitly routes pre-mutation blocking guards through RequestValidatorRegistry (validators run before transaction.Run; service/customer.go:57-59 DeleteCustomer calls requestValidatorRegistry.ValidateDeleteCustomer before opening the transaction). The canonical entitlement guard for exactly this rule is already implemented as a RequestValidator (entitlement/validators/customer/validator.go).
deep_findings.message: A pre-mutation blocking guard (reject delete if entitlements exist) is implemented as a customer PreDelete ServiceHook instead of a customer.RequestValidator, eroding the documented separation that validators block before the transaction and hooks react inside it. Move the check to the RequestValidator path (it already exists in entitlement/validators/customer).

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: pf_0010 — FK-less LedgerCustomerAccount referential integrity must be enforced in application code (trade-off violation_signal: 'relying on a database FK to catch a dangling ledger account_id/customer_id'); decision: 'noop/ + app-level reconcile because LedgerCustomerAccount has no FK to Customer/LedgerAccount'
deep_findings.evidence: CustomerLedgerHook only implements PostCreate (hooks.go:43-59), which provisions ledger accounts and writes ledger_customer_account rows (resolvers/adapter/repo.go:48 CreateCustomerAccount). There is no PreDelete/PostDelete that removes or reconciles those rows: a repo-wide grep finds no DeleteCustomerAccount, no LedgerCustomerAccount Delete/soft-delete, and no integrity/reconcile check anywhere in openmeter/ledger. customer/adapter/customer.go DeleteCustomer is a soft-delete (SetDeletedAt) that never touches LedgerCustomerAccount (which is not an Ent edge, so cascade-delete does not cover it). The hook's own comment (hooks.go:15) admits the gap: '// TODO: these hooks should be added to customer lifecycle'. LedgerCustomerAccount.account_id/customer_id are FK-less by design, so nothing at the DB level catches the resulting dangling rows.
deep_findings.message: pf_0010 is realized: the customer→ledger link table has no deletion or reconciliation path. Provisioning is wired (PostCreate) but the matching cleanup/integrity guard the FK-less design depends on does not exist, so customer deletion leaves dangling ledger_customer_account rows with no DB-level guard. Add a customer-delete-side ledger reconcile (PreDelete/PostDelete or a reconciliation job) and an integrity test asserting every account_id/customer_id resolves to a live row.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Re-entrancy guard via NewContextWithSkipSubjectCustomer (customer pattern: 'Re-entrancy guard for hooks that call back into customer.Service'; subject/service/hooks decision: 'Skip flag ... when a reverse hook is also registered')
deep_findings.evidence: subjectCustomerHook.PostDelete (subjectcustomer.go:102-118) calls p.provisioner.customer.UpdateCustomer(ctx, ...) directly WITHOUT wrapping ctx in subjectservicehooks.NewContextWithSkipSubjectCustomer — unlike EnsureCustomer in the same file which does wrap it at lines 437 and 483. In cmd/server BOTH reverse hooks are registered (wire.go:119 NewCustomerSubjectServiceHook = subject->customer, and wire.go:153 NewSubjectCustomerHook = customer->subject), exactly the bidirectional topology the skip-guard exists to protect. So a subject delete fires subjectCustomerHook.PostDelete -> customer.UpdateCustomer -> customerSubjectHook.PostUpdate (the reverse hook) -> EnsureSubjects, re-entering the cross-domain provisioning inside the same transaction with no skip flag set.
deep_findings.message: subjectCustomerHook.PostDelete calls customer.UpdateCustomer without the NewContextWithSkipSubjectCustomer guard, so with both reverse hooks registered in cmd/server it re-enters the customer->subject provisioning hook. Every other callback into the opposite service in these files sets the skip flag; this one omits it, eroding the established loop-prevention pattern. Wrap the ctx with NewContextWithSkipSubjectCustomer before the UpdateCustomer call.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Config struct + Validate() + constructor (customer/subject hooks pattern: 'Every hook is built via a Config with a Validate() (mandatory nil-dependency checks); the constructor calls Validate() first')
deep_findings.evidence: NewCustomerLedgerHook (hooks.go:36-41) constructs &customerLedgerHook{config: config} without any config.Validate() call, and CustomerLedgerHookConfig has no Validate() method. Every sibling cross-domain hook validates its config in the constructor: NewEntitlementValidatorHook (entitlementvalidator.go:38-41), NewSubjectCustomerHook (subjectcustomer.go:171-174), NewCustomerSubjectHook (subjectcustomer.go:81-89 in subject/service/hooks). If config.Service or config.Tracer is nil, PostCreate (hooks.go:43-50) nil-panics at first customer create instead of failing fast at wiring time.
deep_findings.message: NewCustomerLedgerHook diverges from the established hook pattern by constructing without a Config.Validate() nil-dependency check that all sibling hooks enforce; nil Service/Tracer surfaces as a runtime panic in PostCreate rather than a wiring-time error. Add a CustomerLedgerHookConfig.Validate() and call it in the constructor.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() everywhere, never time.Now() (openmeter decision/anti-pattern: 'Using time.Now() instead of clock.Now() ... breaks time freezing and deterministic billing tests')
deep_findings.evidence: openmeter/customer/httpdriver/customer.go:113 and :637 build subscription.ListSubscriptionsInput{ActiveAt: lo.ToPtr(time.Now())} using the stdlib time.Now() instead of pkg/clock.Now(). The customer adapter establishes the project convention (customer/adapter/customer.go:41 now := clock.Now().UTC()), and the customer domain uses clock.Now() pervasively (e.g. Customer.IsDeleted uses clock.Now()). Using raw time.Now() in the request path makes 'active at now' non-deterministic under clock.FreezeTime and inconsistent with the rest of the customer flow.
deep_findings.message: Two customer v1 handlers compute the 'active subscriptions at now' cutoff with stdlib time.Now() instead of clock.Now(), diverging from the codebase-wide clock.Now() convention and breaking time-frozen test determinism for this path. Replace both with clock.Now().

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: Ingest Collector with DeduplicatingCollector wrapper (openmeter/ingest/CLAUDE.md: '(false, err) means the deduplicator itself failed — the event is still forwarded to avoid data loss; only (false, nil) is a true duplicate')
deep_findings.evidence: DeduplicatingCollector.Ingest (dedupe.go:20-31) calls d.Deduplicator.IsUnique and, when err != nil (lines 22-24), returns the error WITHOUT calling d.Collector.Ingest — so the event is dropped, not forwarded. redisdedupe.IsUnique returns (false, err) on any Redis failure (redisdedupe.go:42, 61, 71), and the wrapper is wired into production via app/common/openmeter_server.go:65. The package's own documented contract (ingest/CLAUDE.md) requires forwarding the event on a deduplicator failure to avoid data loss; the implementation does the opposite, so a transient Redis outage silently drops ingested usage events instead of forwarding them.
deep_findings.message: DeduplicatingCollector.Ingest drops events when the deduplicator itself errors, contradicting its documented data-loss-avoidance contract ('forward on (false, err)'). On a Redis outage redisdedupe.IsUnique returns (false, err), so usage events are lost at ingest rather than forwarded. Forward the event (or fail-open) when IsUnique returns a non-nil error; only treat (false, nil) as a true duplicate.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: clock.Now() everywhere, never time.Now(), for deterministic billing/usage tests (openmeter/CLAUDE.md anti-pattern: 'Using time.Now() instead of clock.Now() ... breaks time freezing'; .claude/rules dev-rules)
deep_findings.evidence: ingest/service.go:80 processEvent sets the CloudEvent's business timestamp with event.SetTime(time.Now().UTC()) for events that arrive without a time. That timestamp becomes the persisted ClickHouse RawEvent.time column (the meter-aggregation time dimension). The sibling producer kafkaingest/collector.go:152 correctly uses clock.Now() for the ingested_at header, and sink/storage.go uses clock.Now() for stored_at — ingest.service is the lone hot-path that stamps a persisted usage time with raw time.Now(), so clock.FreezeTime in tests cannot control the event time of timestamp-less ingested events.
deep_findings.message: ingest.service.processEvent stamps timestamp-less events with time.Now().UTC() (service.go:80) instead of clock.Now(); this value is persisted as the ClickHouse event time used for meter aggregation. Sibling pipeline code (kafkaingest collector, sink storage) uses clock.Now(). Replace with clock.Now().UTC() so event-time is deterministic under clock.FreezeTime.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: pf-008 (ctxless-callback-background): helpers omitting context.Context force context.Background(), severing cancellation/deadlines/OTel spans/Ent tx — AGENTS.md 'never introduce context.Background()/TODO() to sidestep missing context propagation'
deep_findings.evidence: stripeAppClient.providerError(err error) (appclient.go:227) takes no context.Context, yet at line 240 calls c.appService.UpdateAppStatus(context.Background(), ...) to flip the app to Unauthorized. Every caller of providerError (DeleteWebhook ctx l.162, GetAccount ctx l.171, GetPaymentMethod ctx l.190, and invoice.go/customer.go/checkout.go/portal.go callers) has a live request-scoped ctx in scope that is dropped. This is the exact pitfall the blueprint names (pf_0008 cites appclient.go:240). The DB write to update app status thereby runs detached from the request ctx, losing the OTel span, cancellation, and any ctx-bound Ent transaction.
deep_findings.message: Thread ctx through providerError: change the signature to providerError(ctx context.Context, err error) and pass the caller's ctx into UpdateAppStatus instead of context.Background(). The client/CLAUDE.md frames this as an 'intentional exception' but the blueprint pitfall pf-008 explicitly flags this site; refactoring the signature is the prescribed fix direction.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: openmeter/CLAUDE.md anti-pattern: 'Using time.Now() instead of clock.Now() ... breaks time freezing' (clock.Now() everywhere for deterministic billing/price tests)
deep_findings.evidence: Reconciler.Reconcile uses now := time.Now().UTC() (reconciler.go:66) and persists it verbatim as llmcost.Price.EffectiveFrom (l.113) via repo.UpsertGlobalPrice. The llmcost adapter itself uses clock.Now() (openmeter/llmcost/adapter/price.go), so the persisted effective-from timestamp on a reconciled global price row bypasses the freezable clock the rest of the domain relies on. This makes reconciler_test assertions on EffectiveFrom non-deterministic and diverges from the domain-wide clock.Now() convention.
deep_findings.message: Use clock.Now() (truncated/UTC as needed) for the persisted EffectiveFrom in the reconciler so global-price effective timestamps participate in clock.FreezeTime; reserve time.Now() for the wall-clock FetchedAt in fetchers.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: portal CLAUDE.md decision: 'AllowedMeterSlugs *[]string: nil means all slugs allowed; non-nil empty means none allowed; callers and the authenticator must distinguish these' + 'AllowedMeterSlugs check must happen even when the meterSlug route param is absent'
deep_findings.evidence: CreateTokenInput.AllowedMeterSlugs is *[]string with the documented nil=all / non-nil-empty=none semantics (service.go:52,82), but PortalTokenClaims.AllowedMeterSlugs is a plain []string (service.go:35), so the nil-vs-empty distinction is lost the moment a token is minted (adapter/token.go:58 dereferences the pointer). The authenticator then gates with `if len(claims.AllowedMeterSlugs) != 0 && !slices.Contains(...)` (authenticator.go:111): a token created with a non-nil empty allowlist (intent: deny ALL meters) has len==0 at validation time, so the check is skipped and ALL meters are allowed — the inverse of the documented contract. The route-param-absent case is also unguarded.
deep_findings.message: Preserve the nil-vs-empty distinction across the JWT boundary (e.g. a presence flag or *[]string in claims) and change the authenticator guard so a non-nil-but-empty allowlist denies every meter, matching the documented 'non-nil empty means none allowed' security contract.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: pf-008 (ctxless-callback-background) / dev-rule: never introduce context.Background() to sidestep missing context propagation
deep_findings.evidence: server.go:213-214 — the v1 OapiRequestValidatorWithOptions Options.ErrorHandler closure has signature func(w http.ResponseWriter, message string, statusCode int) (no *http.Request), so it substitutes models.NewStatusProblem(context.Background(), errors.New(message), statusCode).Respond(w). models.NewStatusProblem reads the request-id from the Chi RequestID middleware ctx, so every v1 request-validation error response is emitted with a background ctx — dropping request-id correlation and any OTel span/cancellation. The inline comment ('the OpenAPI 3 filter library doesn't support context changes') acknowledges the limitation but the ErrorHandler still hard-codes Background().
deep_findings.message: This is the canonical pf-008 instance named in the blueprint pitfalls. The kin-openapi Options.ErrorHandler signature omits *http.Request; per pf-008 the fix is to thread the request-scoped ctx (e.g. wrap OapiRequestValidatorWithOptions in an outer middleware that captures r.Context() and surfaces validation errors through errorHandlerReply, which already uses r.Context()). As written, every v1 validation-error problem+json loses request-id correlation.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: pf-006a-worker-namespace-precondition / Namespace Manager fan-out (multi-tenancy provisioning); 'cmd/server is the only binary that registers namespace handlers'
deep_findings.evidence: main.go:86 calls app.LedgerAccountResolver.EnsureBusinessAccounts(ctx, app.NamespaceManager.GetDefaultNamespace()) and main.go:93 calls app.AppRegistry.SandboxProvisioner(ctx, GetDefaultNamespace()) directly after Migrate(), with no RegisterHandler call and no fail-fast assertion that the default namespace and its handler-provisioned subsystems (ledger accounts, ClickHouse table, Kafka ingest topic) actually exist. GetDefaultNamespace() only returns the configured default-namespace string (namespace.go:96) — it does not verify provisioning. Only cmd/server registers namespace.Handler implementations (cmd/server/main.go:110-126) and calls CreateDefaultNamespace; nothing in the worker or the Helm chart enforces cmd/server-before-worker boot order.
deep_findings.message: The billing-worker performs namespace-scoped provisioning on an implicit cross-binary boot-order contract (cmd/server must have provisioned the default namespace first). On a fresh database where billing-worker boots before cmd/server, it operates on an unprovisioned namespace. Add a fail-fast precondition check (e.g. assert the default namespace + required subsystems exist) before EnsureBusinessAccounts/SandboxProvisioner, or extract a shared idempotent post-Migrate provisioning function, per pf-006a.

deep_findings.decision_or_pattern: di-001 / 'Noop (never nil) for optional integrations' — Wire providers for disabled optional features must return a noop implementation, not nil
deep_findings.evidence: NewCreditGrantService (creditgrant.go:21-35) returns `return nil, nil` at line 22 when billingRegistry.Charges == nil (credits disabled), rather than returning creditgrant.NewNoopService() as the noop-for-disabled-feature decision requires. The companion v3 server constructor compensates by re-assigning creditGrantService = creditgrant.NewNoopService() when !Credits.Enabled (api/v3/server/server.go:300), and routes.go guards each credit route with `s.CreditGrantService == nil` — but the bare nil from this Wire provider is exactly the di-001 anti-pattern ('returning nil instead of a noop struct for a disabled optional feature — callers receive the interface and panic on nil'). The nil is also injected into router.Config.CreditGrantService (cmd/server/main.go:166) and v1 router.Config (router.go:110); it is safe only because no v1 endpoint dispatches to it and v3 substitutes the noop.
deep_findings.message: Return creditgrant.NewNoopService() (the established noop) instead of nil from this provider so the credits-disabled contract is self-consistent at the Wire layer rather than relying on a downstream re-assignment in the v3 server constructor. The current shape makes the safety of the nil dependent on a separate file's guard, which is brittle if a future caller consumes app.CreditGrantService directly.
